I give the safe answer—bad breakup—but I swallow the longer story: David, the three years, the wife, the vow to never be powerless again. <error>He nods. “Three years is a long time to spend with someone else’s husband,” he says softly. “David shouldn’t have made you carry that.”</error>
story:
Friday-night hum, candlelight, Jules in reading glasses recommending Sancerre. I smooth my black dress and pretend I’m not nervous. He’s a molecular biologist with a soft laugh; I’m a digital marketer who can talk for hours about color psychology. We click over the overlap—science and marketing both manipulate variables—and the word “manipulate” lingers like a seed.

Third date honesty: he teases about Googling; I admit to LinkedIn creeping. He’s published, won a teaching award, and, with three sisters, learned respectful charm. He calls the dress the right choice. Starters arrive—my burrata, his carpaccio—and we glide through travel, jokes, and small confessions. He asks why someone like me is single and jokes I might be a bone collector. I give the safe answer—bad breakup—but I swallow the longer story: David, the three years, the wife, the vow to never be powerless again. He nods. “Three years is a long time to spend with someone else’s husband,” he says softly. “David shouldn’t have made you carry that.”

He offers his own wound: an ex-fiancée who walked three months before the wedding. He wanted Sunday mornings, farmer’s markets, kids someday; she didn’t. He asks what I want. The profile answer is “real commitment,” but the truth slips out: I want to not get hurt. He says hurting me is the last thing on his mind and tops up my wine.

We trade worst-date disasters—his involves fruit flies and a drink in the face—and he jokes I’m either genuinely interested or an excellent faker. The words hit hard. I’ve been performing for weeks: the version of me that’s curious, steady, unbroken. I ask if he ever feels like he’s acting. He says yes—after Rachel left, he tried to be “fine” until therapy, boxing, and trash TV let him be not fine, then better. He asks if I’m pretending.

The mains land with my answer. Maybe. I don’t know how to stop. He says he likes the performance but suspects he’d like what’s underneath too.

Here is where I’m supposed to close the trap I built. Instead, something cracks. I tell him I’m not who he thinks I am. I chose him because he seemed kind and steady and likely to fall. I wanted power, to make someone feel what I felt.

He sets down his fork and really looks at me. He calls me hurt, not terrible. We all calculate at first, he says; the difference is I chose honesty. Brave and stupid often look the same. I tell him to run. He doesn’t. He offers three exits: a walk, an Uber, or dessert and the truth.

Tears come. He squeezes my hand and doesn’t flinch. He says someone once gave him the same chance. He jokes about RNA and fruit flies until I laugh for real. This becomes the weirdest third date ever and maybe the first unplanned moment I’ve had in months.

We choose dessert—tiramisu—and I promise a long, ugly story. He says good, he likes long stories. Maybe there’s something real under the armor and the scheming, he says, and nobody here has to be perfect or fine. We order sweetness and trade the script for honesty, two walking wounds willing to see what happens when we stop trying to win and start trying to be human.
